title: Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema — When to Use What?
page_content: Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema — When to Use What?Garvita Saini4 min read·2 hours ago--ListenSharePress enter or click to view image in full sizeIf you are starting with data engineering, analytics, or BI tools like Power BI or Tableau, you will quickly come across two important concepts:Star SchemaSnowflake SchemaThese are ways to organize data inside a data warehouse. Let’s understand them in the simplest way possible.Understanding Fact and Dimension TablesTo really understand Star and Snowflake schemas, you need to understand the two building blocks behind them: fact tables and dimension tables.A fact table stores the core business events — the measurable data you actually want to analyze. Think of things like revenue, number of orders, clicks, or transactions. Each row in a fact table typically represents a single event, such as one order or one user action. These tables tend to grow quickly and can become very large over time.On the other hand, dimension tables provide the context around those events. They answer questions like who, what, where, and when. For example, instead of just having a product_id in your fact table, a dimension table helps you understand that the product is “Nike Running Shoes” in the “Footwear” category.The real value comes from how these two work together. A fact table might tell you that a sale happened, but dimension tables help you understand what was sold, who bought it, and when it occurred.What Is a Star Schema?A Star Schema is the simplest and most widely used data warehouse schema. It consists of:A central fact table (contains measurable metrics like sales, revenue, quantity)Multiple denormalized dimension tables (store descriptive attributes like customer, product, time)Press enter or click to view image in full sizeExample:Fact_Sales (transaction_id, product_id, customer_id, date_id, revenue)Dim_Product (product_name, category, brand)Dim_Customer (name, location, segment)Dim_Date (day, month, year)Why Star Schema Works So WellStar Schema is popular for a reason. It prioritizes simplicity and performance. Because dimension tables are denormalized:Queries require fewer joinsDashboards run fasterAnalysts can understand the model easilyBI tools perform betterIn tools like Snowflake or Google BigQuery, this translates directly into faster query execution and lower complexity. That’s why most BI dashboards you see today are powered by star schemas — even if you don’t realize it.What is a Snowflake Schema?A Snowflake Schema is a more normalized version of the star schema. It consists of:A central fact tableNormalized dimension tables split into multiple related tablesPress enter or click to view image in full sizeSnowflake SchemaExample:Instead of one Dim_Product table:Dim_Product (product_id, product_name, brand_id, category_id)Dim_Brand (brand_id, brand_name)Dim_Category (category_id, category_name)Why Snowflake Schema ExistsSnowflake Schema optimizes for data organization and storage efficiency. By normalizing data:Redundancy is reducedData consistency improvesUpdates become easier to manageStorage can be optimized (especially in traditional systems)It’s a more “correct” design from a data modeling perspective. But that doesn’t mean it’s always the best choice.Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema (Quick Comparison)Press enter or click to view image in full sizeAdvantages and Disadvantages: Star vs Snowflake SchemaPress enter or click to view image in full sizeFinal ThoughtsThe Star vs Snowflake debate is not about right or wrong. It’s about context.If your goal is to build fast, scalable, and user-friendly analytics systems: Start with Star Schema.If your goal is strict normalization and data consistency: Snowflake Schema has its place.In real-world companies, Star Schema is used much more often because it works better for analytics and dashboards. However, many systems use a mix of both, depending on the need.If you found this useful, feel free to support and follow for more.
